pattern: Greedily reserving your ladders for the largest climbs seen so far, and paying bricks only for the smaller ones, keeps every prefix decision optimal without ever needing to look ahead.
hints: At every uphill step you must spend either a ladder or bricks equal to the height difference -- so as you move forward, keep track of every climb you've paid for with a ladder so far.

If you're currently holding more ladder-covered climbs than you actually have ladders for, it's always safe to convert your cheapest ladder-covered climb back into bricks, since the biggest climbs are the ones most worth saving a ladder for.

A min-heap of the climbs currently assigned a ladder lets you always evict the smallest one back to bricks the moment you run out of ladders; the instant the brick total would go negative, the previous building is as far as you can go.

edge_inputs.description: heights never increase, so no bricks or ladders are ever needed
a single building, nothing to climb
bricks exactly cover the one climb needed
bricks fall one short of the climb needed
more ladders than climbs, so bricks are never touched
maximum possible bricks with no ladders
ladders equal to the array length, covering every possible climb

stress.description: large random skyline with random brick and ladder budgets
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    heights = [rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    bricks = rng.randint(0, 1000000000)
    ladders = rng.randint(0, n)
    return [heights, bricks, ladders]
